annotations.content: When we were cruising down 134 and hit the 1806 we saw a subquarter high patrol there and decided to strike up a conversation to educate them about the about the indigenous issues. Most of the officers that I have spoken with and asked about the idigenous issues had no clue on why they were there. They were just there on assignement, they are on duty.
Hey! We are just doing a documentary here, is there anything we need to know about this road? Is it safe for us, like that?
There's a protest up here, that's why you are up here, ain't it?
O we're just documenting, you know like...
Oh yeah... you guys... you're just up here for.. just decided to come up here? To come up here in the middle of nowhere in North Dakota?
Let me tell you something. We've been here for over 5000 years. We've been here predating western contact. Yeah, we've got all kind of reasons to travel through here.
So you just came here now and decided you document..?

I've been up here many times working with the youth (and) that risk (...) suicide, sexual abuse, all those issues that are happening... From over the last 500 years, we are doing historical trauma training. You know you're on, what is it, sacred sites that are all aroung here right?
I've heard there are sacred sites... What makes this road to you sacred, I guess I'm kinda curious since you said it's a sacred site, I've just...

Oh, how about this: "[indigenous language] - we are power in place, where we pray right now is sacred. It does not have to be destinated as sacred by you or anyone else in this country. It's where we pray.

You seem pretty reasonable. But I have seen things from local martin (?) county, I personally experienced things from them that just, just fells just like violations of the constitution, just feels so..
What, what.. constitutional rights do you feel you have violated?

Last month on the sixth I was filming and I started flying a drone. They came up and tried to steel my drone. And I was totally cooperative. Now if he had been a neutral officer you know, he would have just came up and politely reminded me, "hey Sir, you need to be at this distance away from the crowd, if you are not gonna listen, you should land it you know, it's the equivalent of jaywalking in the drone world. While this was happening they were allowing the spray painting on the blades of the maschines to happen and were'nt saying anywthing on it, so it's like they are creating a narrative that they want, allowing actions that they want to happen to make us seem unlawful meanwhile targeting me because I'm filming.
What kind of right do you feel has been violated?
My right to film, my right to document, the freedom of press.
Who do you work for, who is the press agency?

They said they are tipping the scales of justice and it's not in our favor and it's not to make a just outcome, it's in effect to deny us justice. So there is a lot of behind the scenes stuff that people need to be aware of right now. What's happening now was gonna set the precident, I believe, for future movements like this. And we all know with Trump coming in there's gonna be more movements like this. Because us, the people, we love the earth, we love eath other, we are not goona stop standing up, so we gotta pay attention, we gotta get the word out, we gotta make sure that our water protectors are protected.
Know your law, know your rights, know your first amendment, your forth amendment. They can not come into your vehicle without a warren: Probable cause. They can't just bully their way.
They had no warrent to grab anything. Now we go.
Did you guys have a warrent to go through that vehicle?
Can we see the warrent? For the vehicle you just went through. They had no warrent, they can't go through the vehicle.

Know the rules and laws. For indian people it's double, we know federal law, we gotta know state law, man it's hard to be indigenous! You know, we gotta know cultural law, protocols... it's not easy! But we do it.
How did you-? Can I see your warrent that you said you -?
You said you had a warrent to go through the vehicle.
Can I see the warrent? That's all I'm asking, can I see the warrent?
You can talk to (the) state's (...) officer and they can discuss that with you, you are free to leave.
Okay. So for the record: You said you had a warrent. That's all I'm asking. I'll leave but you gotta answer that question.
You can discuss that with the state's attourney. You're free to leave.
You can look that up as well, I don't need to give you any information.
Shouldn't you give me some paperwork? You've stolen property.
We haven't stolen anything, we simply ceased evidence of a crime. You're free to leave, good day.
Where was the crime at? Can you tell me where the crime was at?
You took property and I'm asking you for correct information. That's all I'm asking for. The attourney is gonna ask you this too. This makes no sense. Which county is 54?
The attourney can ask the questions, okay?

All I'm asking is for names, and that's as simple as it gets, Sir. I'll leave but, just what I'm saying is, all I need is a name, and that shouldn't be a problem. Well thank you guys for.. I don't even know what this is.. All I'm asking for is what county is this in, what the badge name is... The name is on here though? What is the name on here?
Good night. Have a nice evening.

I don't know why they couldn't just politely tell you what was going on, explain the piece of paper that they just gave to you, I don't know why that's so difficult. I thought that police were in the business of trying to serve people and that's clearly not what's going on.
We can also ask what does it mean to be harrassed, you know, by an officer. If they follow you, what's the distance of being harrassed, just in your professional opinion?
They can follow you as long as they want just to follow you? What if we stop and they stop and then we go and they go and we stop and they stop?
I don't know who your name. I don't know who you are, what's your name?
I know who you are, what's up?
Just wondering what's going on, you guys are... following us, you know... just standing there..
That's kind of quite a bit of patrol... Is there something that we need to be safe from? For our safety? No?
No, we're just out patrolling... Have a good day.
What are you guys looking for?
What do you think of that?
They knew me by my name! You know.. That's pretty, uh... that's pretty crazy.
Yeah, well, you know, you... yeah.
For being a native journalist, that's that!
So they're keeping a close eye on people through...
So we go here again. We go on live here guys. Ho-ho, relatives! [indigenous language] Have a good day!
Martin county is not the problem. They are part of the problem. And they become the distraction from the oil company.
Right. They become a distraction... By money.. and..

And when I was documenting these guys, it wasn't who I wanted to document. Because while they were doing what they were doing the Dakota Access Pipeline was moving all their equipment in.. And that's when I first realized this was a distraction.
That's why I'm a fan of live feeds. Rather then a news clip. You know a news clip is cut here and there.. to a live feed is a full length video. You can't argue with that.

I think the real danger is that the power is trying to elevate the status of what we're trying to do here as domestic terrorism that becomes a justification for more vicious and massive repression. Because the human rights have already been violated on a number of different levels. They're using domestic resources on behalf of an international corporate entity to contain and repress sovereign nations as well as citizens of the United States who are in solidarity with sovereign nations. But as I said, the real danger is that for them to begin to consolidate their resources and treat this movement as if it's a domestic terrorist movement. it is just another moment in the increasing fascist policies of this government both in terms of what's going on here but also as we move into a Trump moment. So it's very very crucial issue. And I just want you to know my brother, you use my name on any occasion to defend the dignity of my precious indigenous brothers and sisters. That's what I'm here for.
That's what I'm here for my brother indeed.
